main_paper.raw: {"methods":["BLIP-3-Video model with explicit temporal encoder","Learnable spatio-temporal pooling as temporal encoder","Sequential models (e.g., Token Turing Machines) as temporal encoder","Space-time attentional pooling"],"problems":["Efficiently representing videos for vision-language models (VLMs) with fewer visual tokens","Capturing temporal information across multiple frames in video for VLMs","Reducing computational cost in video-based VLMs by minimizing the number of visual tokens"],"datasets":["MSVD-QA"],"metrics":["Accuracy (on video question-answering tasks)"],"results":[{"metric":"MSVD-QA accuracy (BLIP3-V-4B-32t)","value":"comparable to state-of-the-art models (e.g., 34B) while using only 32 tokens"},{"metric":"MSVD-QA accuracy (BLIP3-V-4B-16t)","value":"successful in open-ended question-answering tasks with only 16 tokens"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of BLIP-3-Video, a compact and efficient VLM for videos with an explicit temporal encoder.","Demonstration that as few as 16-32 visual tokens are sufficient to represent a video for question-answering tasks, compared to thousands in prior models.","Exploration and implementation of learnable spatio-temporal pooling and sequential models (e.g., Token Turing Machines) as temporal encoders in VLMs.","Provision of a fundamental alternative to brute-force collection of all frame-level visual tokens in video VLMs."]}

methods: BLIP-3-Video model with explicit temporal encoder
Learnable spatio-temporal pooling as temporal encoder
Sequential models (e.g., Token Turing Machines) as temporal encoder
Space-time attentional pooling
problems: Efficiently representing videos for vision-language models (VLMs) with fewer visual tokens
Capturing temporal information across multiple frames in video for VLMs
Reducing computational cost in video-based VLMs by minimizing the number of visual tokens

results.value: comparable to state-of-the-art models (e.g., 34B) while using only 32 tokens
successful in open-ended question-answering tasks with only 16 tokens

novelty_claims: Introduction of BLIP-3-Video, a compact and efficient VLM for videos with an explicit temporal encoder.
Demonstration that as few as 16-32 visual tokens are sufficient to represent a video for question-answering tasks, compared to thousands in prior models.
Exploration and implementation of learnable spatio-temporal pooling and sequential models (e.g., Token Turing Machines) as temporal encoders in VLMs.
Provision of a fundamental alternative to brute-force collection of all frame-level visual tokens in video VLMs.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["PruMerge: adaptive visual token reduction strategy based on attention sparsity and token importance scoring (using Interquartile Range)","Clustering and merging of selected visual tokens using k-nearest neighbor and weighted averaging","PruMerge+: spatial-uniform sampling to supplement unpruned tokens for more comprehensive token selection"],"problems":["High computational cost of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) due to large number of visual tokens and quadratic complexity of Transformer attention","Redundancy among visual tokens leading to inefficiency in methods: PruMerge: adaptive visual token reduction strategy based on attention sparsity and token importance scoring (using Interquartile Range)
Clustering and merging of selected visual tokens using k-nearest neighbor and weighted averaging
PruMerge+: spatial-uniform sampling to supplement unpruned tokens for more comprehensive token selection
problems: High computational cost of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) due to large number of visual tokens and quadratic complexity of Transformer attention
Redundancy among visual tokens leading to inefficiency in inference and training
Maintaining performance of LMMs while reducing the number of visual tokens
metrics: Number of visual tokens (token reduction ratio)
Performance on visual question-answering and reasoning tasks (e.g., accuracy, benchmark scores)

results.value: 14x reduction (from 576 to ~32 tokens per image on average)
4-10x reduction
Comparable to baseline (retaining all 576 tokens) across diverse VQA and reasoning benchmarks

novelty_claims: Proposes PruMerge, a novel adaptive visual token reduction strategy that leverages attention sparsity to select and merge important visual tokens, significantly reducing token count without sacrificing performance.
Introduces a method to dynamically select and merge visual tokens based on information density, using IQR scoring and k-nearest neighbor clustering.
Demonstrates that LMMs can operate with only 5.5% of the original visual tokens (about 32 tokens) while maintaining comparable performance.
PruMerge+ further supplements token selection with spatial-uniform sampling, ensuring comprehensive token coverage and minimal performance degradation.
Shows that PruMerge can be applied to both image and video modalities, and can be integrated at inference time without retraining.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["MiniGPT4-Video: a multimodal LLM for video understanding that processes interleaved visual and textual tokens","Concatenation of every four adjacent visual tokens to reduce token count while mitigating information loss","Incorporation of subtitles for each frame, representing each frame as a combination of visual tokens (from a visual encoder) and text tokens (from LLM tokenizers)","Evaluation using both open-ended and multiple-choice zeroshot methodologies"],"problems":["Enabling LLMs to understand and process video data, which includes temporal visual information and associated text","Mitigating information loss when encoding video frames for LLMs","Improving the ability of LLMs to answer queries involving both visual and textual components in videos"],"datasets":["MSVD","MSRVTT","TGIF","TVQA","ActivityNet"],"metrics":["Accuracy (on MSVD, MSRVTT, TGIF, TVQA)","Video-ChatGPT benchmark metrics: information correctness, detail orientation, contextual understanding, temporal comprehension, consistency"],"results":[{"metric":"MSVD Accuracy Improvement","value":"4.22% over SOTA (7B)"},{"metric":"MSRVTT Accuracy Improvement","value":"1.13% over SOTA (7B)"},{"metric":"TGIF Accuracy Improvement","value":"20.82% over SOTA (7B)"},{"metric":"TVQA Accuracy Improvement","value":"13.1% over SOTA (7B)"}],"novelty_claims":["First to extend MiniGPT-v2 to video understanding by processing sequences of frames with interleaved visual and textual tokens","Proposes a token concatenation strategy (every four adjacent visual tokens) to reduce token count while preserving information","Incorporates subtitles at the frame level, allowing richer multimodal representation for each frame","Demonstrates significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods on multiple video understanding benchmarks"]}

Evaluation using both open-ended and multiple-choice zeroshot methodologies

methods: SlowFast-LLaVA (SF-LLaVA): a training-free video large language model using a two-stream SlowFast input design
Slow pathway: extracts features at low frame rate with high spatial detail
Fast pathway: extracts features at high frame rate with aggressive spatial pooling to focus on motion cues
Feature extraction via visual encoder (e.g., CLIP-L) and visual-language adaptor
Concatenation of Slow and Fast pathway features as input to LLM (LLaVA-NeXT)

novelty_claims: Proposes a training-free Video LLM (SF-LLaVA) that jointly captures detailed spatial semantics and long-range temporal context via a two-stream SlowFast input design

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["TEmporal-Spatial Token Aggregation (TESTA): an adaptive token aggregation method that merges similar frames (temporal aggregation) and similar patches within frames (spatial aggregation) during video encoding","A pre-trained video-language model equipped with a divided space-time token aggregation module in each video encoder block","Use of bipartite matching algorithm for selecting and aggregating similar tokens via mean pooling"],"problems":["High computational cost and inefficiency of video encoding for long-form video-language understanding due to large number of visual tokens and spatiotemporal redundancy","Difficulty in capturing complex temporal and spatial relationships in long-form videos without sacrificing performance or efficiency"],"datasets":["QuerYD","Condensed Movie","Three additional unnamed datasets for paragraph-to-video retrieval and long-form VideoQA tasks (not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["R@1 (Recall at 1)","Computing efficiency (speedup factor)"],"results":[{"metric":"Visual token reduction","value":"75%"},{"metric":"Computing efficiency improvement","value":"1.7x"},{"metric":"R@1 on QuerYD","value":"+13.7"},{"metric":"R@1 on Condensed Movie","value":"+6.5"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes TESTA, a method that adaptively aggregates similar frames and patches to reduce visual tokens by 75% without sacrificing end-to-end spatiotemporal modeling","TESTA operates aggregation separately in temporal and spatial dimensions, improving efficiency and scalability for long-form video encoding","Aggregation is parameter-free, parallelizable, and interpretable, with aggregation trajectories that can be tracked and recovered","Demonstrates significant efficiency and performance gains on long-form video-language tasks compared to prior methods"]}

methods: TEmporal-Spatial Token Aggregation (TESTA): an adaptive token aggregation method that merges similar frames (temporal aggregation) and similar patches within frames (spatial aggregation) during video encoding
A pre-trained video-language model equipped with a divided space-time token aggregation module in each video encoder block
Use of bipartite matching algorithm for selecting and aggregating similar tokens via mean pooling
problems: High computational cost and inefficiency of video encoding for long-form video-language understanding due to large number of visual tokens and spatiotemporal redundancy
Difficulty in capturing complex temporal and spatial relationships in long-form videos without sacrificing performance or efficiency
datasets: Three additional unnamed datasets for paragraph-to-video retrieval and long-form VideoQA tasks (not specified in the provided content)
novelty_claims: Proposes TESTA, a method that adaptively aggregates similar frames and patches to reduce visual tokens by 75% without sacrificing end-to-end spatiotemporal modeling
TESTA operates aggregation separately in temporal and spatial dimensions, improving efficiency and scalability for long-form video encoding
Aggregation is parameter-free, parallelizable, and interpretable, with aggregation trajectories that can be tracked and recovered
Demonstrates significant efficiency and performance gains on long-form video-language tasks compared to prior methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Image Grid Vision Language Model (IG-VLM): Convert a video into a single composite image by arranging sampled frames in a grid layout, then use a single pre-trained Vision Language Model (VLM) for zero-shot video question answering (VQA) without any video-specific training.","Extension method for handling long videos by adapting the image grid approach."],"problems":["Zero-shot Video Question Answering (VQA) without requiring video-specific training.","Bridging the gap between video data and language models using only image-based VLMs.","Overcoming limitations of existing VideoLMs (limited video data, token bottleneck) and multi-stage foundation models (information loss in text conversion, modality gap)."],"datasets":["Ten zero-shot VQA benchmarks (five open-ended and five multiple-choice). Specific dataset names are not provided in the excerpt."],"metrics":["Accuracy (normalized to a maximum score of 100 for text generation benchmarks)"],"results":[{"metric":"Number of benchmarks where IG-VLM outperforms SOTA","value":"9 out of 10"},{"metric":"Open-ended VQA benchmarks (IG-VLM performance)","value":"Outperforms existing methods in 5 out of 5 benchmarks"},{"metric":"Multiple-choice VQA benchmarks (IG-VLM performance)","value":"Outperforms existing methods in 4 out of 5 benchmarks"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a simple yet novel strategy of converting videos into image grids to enable direct use of a single VLM for video understanding, without any video-data training.","Demonstrates that a single VLM, when fed an image grid, can outperform state-of-the-art methods on zero-shot VQA benchmarks, challenging the need for complex VideoLMs or multi-stage approaches.","Provides an extension for handling long videos within the image grid framework, further improving performance."]}

methods: Image Grid Vision Language Model (IG-VLM): Convert a video into a single composite image by arranging sampled frames in a grid layout, then use a single pre-trained Vision Language Model (VLM) for zero-shot video question answering (VQA) without any video-specific training.
Extension method for handling long videos by adapting the image grid approach.
problems: Zero-shot Video Question Answering (VQA) without requiring video-specific training.
Bridging the gap between video data and language models using only image-based VLMs.
Overcoming limitations of existing VideoLMs (limited video data, token bottleneck) and multi-stage foundation models (information loss in text conversion, modality gap).
datasets: Ten zero-shot VQA benchmarks (five open-ended and five multiple-choice). Specific dataset names are not provided in the excerpt.
metrics: Accuracy (normalized to a maximum score of 100 for text generation benchmarks)

results.metric: Number of benchmarks where IG-VLM outperforms SOTA
results.value: 9 out of 10
Outperforms existing methods in 5 out of 5 benchmarks
Outperforms existing methods in 4 out of 5 benchmarks

novelty_claims: Proposes a simple yet novel strategy of converting videos into image grids to enable direct use of a single VLM for video understanding, without any video-data training.
Demonstrates that a single VLM, when fed an image grid, can outperform state-of-the-art methods on zero-shot VQA benchmarks, challenging the need for complex VideoLMs or multi-stage approaches.
Provides an extension for handling long videos within the image grid framework, further improving performance.

methods: Flamingo Visual Language Model (VLM): a model that bridges pretrained vision-only and language-only models via novel architectural components, enabling the ingestion of arbitrarily interleaved visual (images/videos) and textual data and producing text as output.
Perceiver-based architecture for visual input: allows the model to process high-resolution images or videos by producing a small, fixed number of visual tokens per image/video.
Few-shot learning via in-context prompting: the model is adapted to new tasks by providing a few input/output examples as prompts, without task-specific fine-tuning.
Training on large-scale multimodal web corpora: Flamingo is trained on web data with interleaved text and images, without using any data annotated for machine learning purposes.
problems: Rapid adaptation to novel multimodal (vision-language) tasks with only a handful of annotated examples (few-shot learning).
Bridging vision-only and language-only pretrained models for multimodal understanding.
Handling sequences of arbitrarily interleaved visual and textual data for open-ended tasks such as visual question answering, captioning, and visual dialogue.
Achieving state-of-the-art performance on multimodal tasks with minimal task-specific data.
datasets: A total of 16 multimodal language and image/video understanding benchmarks (not all named in the excerpt)
metrics: State-of-the-art (SOTA) comparison (relative performance to prior SOTA)
Open-ended generation metrics (e.g., for captioning, VQA)

results.value: Flamingo sets a new state of the art on 16 multimodal tasks in few-shot learning.
On 6 out of 16 tasks, Flamingo surpasses the fine-tuned SOTA using only 32 task-specific examples (about 1000x less data).
With more annotation, Flamingo sets new SOTA on VQAv2, VATEX, VizWiz, MSRVTTQA, and HatefulMemes.

novelty_claims: Introduction of a family of Visual Language Models (Flamingo) that can perform a wide range of multimodal tasks from only a few examples, via in-context few-shot learning.
Novel architecture that bridges pretrained vision and language models, enabling the model to handle arbitrarily interleaved visual and textual data and generate open-ended text outputs.
Ability to ingest both images and videos as input, thanks to a Perceiver-based visual module.
Training exclusively on large-scale web data with interleaved text and images, without any machine learning-annotated data.
Demonstration that a single model can achieve SOTA on a wide range of tasks with orders of magnitude less task-specific data than previous approaches.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FastV: a plug-and-play dynamic image token pruning method for LVLMs that prunes visual tokens after early layers based on adaptive attention patterns"],"problems":["Inefficient attention computation over visual tokens in deep layers of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs)","High computational cost and inference latency in LVLMs due to redundant visual token processing","Balancing computational efficiency and performance in LVLMs, especially for deployment on edge devices"],"datasets":["Nocaps","Flickr30K","A-OKVQA","MMMU","PCA-Bench","OCR-VQA","Jang et al. (2017) video dataset","Xu et al. (2017a;b) video datasets","MME","MMVet","SeedBench"],"metrics":["FLOPs (Floating Point Operations)","Latency","Average performance on vision-language tasks (e.g., accuracy, score)"],"results":[{"metric":"FLOPs reduction (LLaVA-1.5-13B)","value":"45x reduction"},{"metric":"Image token pruning after layer 2","value":"50% tokens pruned without sacrificing average performance"},{"metric":"Latency (A-OKVQA, LLaVA-13B with FastV vs LLaVA-7B)","value":"LLaVA-13B with FastV achieves lower latency than LLaVA-7B while maintaining superior performance"}],"novelty_claims":["First to identify and analyze the inefficient visual attention phenomena in deep layers of prevailing LVLMs.","Propose FastV, a plug-and-play, dynamic image token pruning method that directly eliminates tokens after early layers based on attention scores, reducing both self-attention and FFN computation.","FastV enables significant reduction in inference cost (FLOPs and latency) without sacrificing performance, and allows higher image resolution processing at no additional inference cost.","FastV is highly customizable and pareto-efficient, allowing trade-offs between computational efficiency and performance."]}

methods: FastV: a plug-and-play dynamic image token pruning method for LVLMs that prunes visual tokens after early layers based on adaptive attention patterns
problems: Inefficient attention computation over visual tokens in deep layers of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs)
High computational cost and inference latency in LVLMs due to redundant visual token processing
Balancing computational efficiency and performance in LVLMs, especially for deployment on edge devices
metrics: Average performance on vision-language tasks (e.g., accuracy, score)

results.value: 45x reduction
50% tokens pruned without sacrificing average performance

results.metric: Latency (A-OKVQA, LLaVA-13B with FastV vs LLaVA-7B)
results.value: LLaVA-13B with FastV achieves lower latency than LLaVA-7B while maintaining superior performance

novelty_claims: First to identify and analyze the inefficient visual attention phenomena in deep layers of prevailing LVLMs.
Propose FastV, a plug-and-play, dynamic image token pruning method that directly eliminates tokens after early layers based on attention scores, reducing both self-attention and FFN computation.
FastV enables significant reduction in inference cost (FLOPs and latency) without sacrificing performance, and allows higher image resolution processing at no additional inference cost.
FastV is highly customizable and pareto-efficient, allowing trade-offs between computational efficiency and performance.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Multimodal Tracking Transformer (MTTR): a Transformer-based end-to-end framework for referring video object segmentation (RVOS) that models the task as a parallel sequence prediction problem","Temporal segment voting scheme: a novel sequence selection strategy to determine which predicted object sequence corresponds to the text query","Use of a single multimodal Transformer to jointly process video and text features","No use of text-related inductive bias modules or mask-refinement post-processing"],"problems":["Referring video object segmentation (RVOS): segmenting the object instance in video frames referred to by a natural language query","Complexity and inefficiency of existing RVOS pipelines, which often require multiple stages and hand-crafted modules","Difficulty in aligning video and text modalities, especially when objects are referred to by actions or require tracking across frames"],"datasets":["A2D-Sentences","JHMDB-Sentences","Refer-YouTube-VOS (public validation set)"],"metrics":["mean Average Precision (mAP)","Processing speed (frames per second)"],"results":[{"metric":"mAP gain on A2D-Sentences","value":"+5.7"},{"metric":"mAP gain on JHMDB-Sentences","value":"+5.0"},{"metric":"Processing speed","value":"76 frames per second"}],"novelty_claims":["First end-to-end Transformer-based framework for RVOS that models the task as a parallel sequence prediction problem","Introduction of a temporal segment voting scheme for sequence selection, enabling the model to focus on relevant video segments for the text query","Elimination of text-related inductive bias modules and mask-refinement post-processing, greatly simplifying the RVOS pipeline","Significant performance improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods on standard RVOS benchmarks"]}

methods: Multimodal Tracking Transformer (MTTR): a Transformer-based end-to-end framework for referring video object segmentation (RVOS) that models the task as a parallel sequence prediction problem
Temporal segment voting scheme: a novel sequence selection strategy to determine which predicted object sequence corresponds to the text query
Use of a single multimodal Transformer to jointly process video and text features
No use of text-related inductive bias modules or mask-refinement post-processing
problems: Referring video object segmentation (RVOS): segmenting the object instance in video frames referred to by a natural language query
Complexity and inefficiency of existing RVOS pipelines, which often require multiple stages and hand-crafted modules
Difficulty in aligning video and text modalities, especially when objects are referred to by actions or require tracking across frames

results.value: 76 frames per second

novelty_claims: First end-to-end Transformer-based framework for RVOS that models the task as a parallel sequence prediction problem
Introduction of a temporal segment voting scheme for sequence selection, enabling the model to focus on relevant video segments for the text query
Elimination of text-related inductive bias modules and mask-refinement post-processing, greatly simplifying the RVOS pipeline
Significant performance improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods on standard RVOS benchmarks

novelty_claims: First to explore BERT-style masked visual modeling in video-text retrieval with dual-encoder models.
Introduces a snapshot video encoder as an evolving tokenizer to produce learning targets for masked video patch prediction.
Applies masked visual modeling in video-text pre-training without extra pre-training stages for cross-modality tokenization.
Demonstrates that masked visual modeling enhances both local video context understanding and fine-grained video-text semantic alignment.

methods: Temporal Token Merging (TempMe): a parameter-efficient and training-inference efficient text-video retrieval architecture
Progressive multi-granularity framework for merging redundant temporal tokens in adjacent video clips
Integration of parameter-efficient tuning with token compression, specifically targeting temporal redundancy
problems: High computational overhead in text-video retrieval due to processing each video frame independently with image encoders
Inefficiency of current parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, which still incur high inference costs
Existing token compression methods are designed for images and do not address temporal redundancy in videos
Lack of dedicated compression algorithms for text-video retrieval models that address temporal redundancy
novelty_claims: First dedicated temporal token compression algorithm for text-video retrieval that directly addresses temporal redundancy across video frames.
TempMe integrates parameter-efficient tuning with temporal token merging, reducing both trainable parameters and inference complexity.
The progressive multi-granularity framework merges redundant temporal tokens step by step, reducing spatio-temporal redundancy and enhancing temporal modeling.
TempMe achieves state-of-the-art efficiency and performance trade-off, outperforming both parameter-efficient and token compression baselines on four benchmarks.

methods: Video-LLaVA: a Large Vision-Language Model that aligns image and video representations into a unified visual feature space before projection to the LLM
Joint training of images and videos to learn a unified visual representation
Use of a shared projection layer after alignment for both images and videos
problems: Lack of unified tokenization and representation for images and videos in LVLMs, leading to misalignment before projection
Difficulty for LLMs to learn multi-modal interactions due to separate feature spaces for images and videos
Inferior performance of existing LVLMs on multi-modal tasks compared to modality-specific expert models
datasets: 5 image benchmarks (not all named, but includes MMBench)

results.value: 5.8% improvement over Video-ChatGPT
9.9% improvement over Video-ChatGPT
18.6% improvement over Video-ChatGPT
10.1% improvement over Video-ChatGPT
6.4% higher than IDEFICS-80B

novelty_claims: Proposes alignment of image and video representations before projection to the LLM, enabling a unified visual representation for both modalities
Establishes a simple but robust LVLM baseline (Video-LLaVA) that can simultaneously handle images and videos
Demonstrates that joint training of images and videos in a unified feature space mutually enhances performance on both modalities
Shows that a unified visual representation outperforms models specifically designed for either images or videos
